text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY INPATIENT CONSULT NOTE
Acute Myeloid Leukemia — Initial Evaluation and Treatment Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old female high school biology teacher presenting with a 3-week history of worsening fatigue, easy bruising, spontaneous gingival bleeding, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. She was referred emergently from her primary care physician after CBC revealed a white blood cell count of 87,400/uL with circulating blasts, hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL, and platelets of 32,000/uL.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a previously healthy 48-year-old woman who reports an approximately 3-week progressive history of constitutional symptoms and hematologic dysfunction. She initially noted profound fatigue that she attributed to work-related stress and what she thought was a viral illness; however, her symptoms worsened significantly over the ensuing weeks. She describes easy bruising appearing spontaneously over her thighs and bilateral lower extremities without antecedent trauma. Approximately 10 days prior to presentation, she noted her gums had begun to swell and bleed spontaneously, particularly during eating and tooth brushing, which she found alarming. She denies prior dental problems. She reports a 12-pound weight loss over the past 3-4 weeks that she attributes to decreased appetite and early satiety. She also endorses mild left-sided upper abdominal fullness and discomfort, which she describes as a sense of heaviness, consistent with splenic enlargement on examination.

She denies fever, night sweats, bone pain, headaches, visual changes, focal neurological symptoms, chest pain, or dyspnea at rest. She has no prior history of hematologic abnormalities, prior cytotoxic exposures, or prior malignancy. She has never received chemotherapy or radiation. She denies any occupational chemical exposures or toxic inhalant exposures.

Her primary care physician obtained a CBC that demonstrated a leukocytosis with WBC 87,400/uL, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, and platelets 32,000/uL. A peripheral blood smear was reviewed and showed approximately 40% circulating blasts with Auer rods. She was immediately referred to our emergency department and subsequently admitted to the inpatient hematology/oncology unit for urgent evaluation and initiation of treatment.

Bone marrow biopsy was performed on the day of admission and confirmed hypercellular marrow at approximately 95% with 72% myeloblasts. Immunophenotyping by flow cytometry confirmed AML (CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+, HLA-DR+). Conventional cytogenetics demonstrated a normal female karyotype (46,XX). Comprehensive myeloid next-generation sequencing identified high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD (exon 14, 33 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.68, VAF 40.2%), as well as co-mutations in DNMT3A (p.R882H, VAF 48.1%), RUNX1 (p.S291fs*16, VAF 43.7%), and TET2 (p.Q916*, VAF 22.4%), with a subclonal PTPN11 p.E76K mutation also identified. No NPM1 mutation was identified. No IDH1/IDH2, TP53, ASXL1, NRAS, KRAS, or KIT mutations were detected. No pathognomonic fusions were present. This molecular profile confers ELN 2022 Adverse risk classification — specifically, high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD in the setting of NPM1-unmutated, normal karyotype AML, compounded by co-occurring RUNX1 frameshift and DNMT3A R882H mutations, which is a particularly high-risk combination.

A staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated mild hepatosplenomegaly (spleen 14.2 cm, liver 17.1 cm) and small bilateral pleural effusions. No discrete tumor masses. Brain MRI showed no leptomeningeal enhancement or parenchymal lesions, arguing against CNS involvement.

The patient has been admitted to the inpatient unit. She is hemodynamically stable. Transfusion support has been initiated. There are no signs of leukostasis (no respiratory distress, no altered mentation). Pre-treatment echocardiogram has been obtained.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Hypothyroidism — diagnosed approximately 12 years ago, on stable levothyroxine, well-controlled with most recent TSH within normal limits per patient report
2. No prior malignancy
3. No prior hematologic disorders (MDS, myeloproliferative neoplasm, aplastic anemia)
4. No prior chemotherapy or radiation therapy
5. No history of cardiac, pulmonary, hepatic, or renal disease

---

SURGICAL HISTORY

Appendectomy in her early 20s. No other surgical procedures.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Chen works as a high school biology teacher. She is married and lives with her husband, who she describes as highly supportive and engaged in her care. She has two teenage children at home. She is a lifelong non-smoker, denies alcohol use beyond rare social occasions (less than 1 drink per week), and denies any illicit substance use. She denies any significant occupational exposures — no pesticides, benzene, or ionizing radiation in her work environment. She has no recent international travel. She was born and raised in the United States. She is of Chinese descent.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

Father: deceased, colon cancer at age 71
Mother: alive, type 2 diabetes, hypertension
One full sibling (brother, age 52): alive and healthy; HLA typing has been sent
No family history of leukemia, lymphoma, myelodysplastic syndrome, or hereditary bone marrow failure syndromes
No known family history of breast, ovarian, or other hereditary cancer syndromes

---

ALLERGIES

NKDA (no known drug allergies)

---

MEDICATIONS (prior to admission)

1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
2. Daily multivitamin

Inpatient medications as of this visit:
- Allopurinol 300 mg PO daily (tumor lysis prophylaxis)
- Aggressive IV hydration (tumor lysis prevention)
- Ondansetron 8 mg IV q8h PRN nausea
- Dexamethasone 8 mg IV with chemotherapy (anti-emetic)
- Packed red blood cells transfused (2 units for Hgb 6.8 g/dL)
- Platelet transfusions for count <10,000/uL
- DVT prophylaxis as clinically appropriate given thrombocytopenia

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Profound fatigue (3 weeks), easy bruising, spontaneous gingival bleeding and gingival swelling, 12-pound unintentional weight loss over 3-4 weeks, decreased appetite, early satiety, left upper quadrant heaviness/fullness, petechiae on bilateral lower extremities noted by patient

Negative: No fever or chills (though afebrile at present; no rigors), no drenching night sweats, no bone pain or arthralgias, no headache, no visual disturbance, no focal weakness or numbness, no dyspnea on exertion at rest, no hemoptysis, no hematuria, no melena or hematochezia, no confusion or cognitive changes, no prior lymphadenopathy

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Temperature 37.2°C, HR 98 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air
Height: 5'4" (163 cm), Weight: 127 lbs (57.6 kg), BSA: approximately 1.6 m²
ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Alert and oriented x3, appears fatigued and mildly anxious. Pale-appearing. In no acute respiratory distress. Cooperative with examination.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Pallor of conjunctiva bilaterally. Sclera anicteric. No oral mucosal ulcers at this time. Gingival hyperplasia is prominent, with visibly boggy, erythematous gingival tissue throughout the upper and lower dental ridges; oozing of blood is noted along the gingival margin, particularly in the molar regions bilaterally, consistent with leukemic gingival infiltration. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Neck: Supple. No lymphadenopathy in anterior or posterior cervical chains, no supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No crackles or wheezes. Breath sounds symmetric.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender to light palpation. Mild left upper quadrant fullness. Spleen tip palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin on deep inspiration. Liver percusses to approximately 17 cm in the right mid-clavicular line; the edge is palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin. No ascites. Active bowel sounds.

Extremities: Multiple petechiae over the bilateral lower extremities from the shins to the thighs. Several ecchymotic areas, up to 6 cm in diameter, on bilateral inner thighs and left forearm. No joint effusions. No peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Skin: Pallor diffusely. Petechiae as described. No rash. No jaundice.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation intact. Gait normal. No cerebellar signs.

Lymph nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy on examination (cervical, axillary, inguinal).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC:
- WBC: 87,400/uL (markedly elevated)
- Differential: ~40% blasts on peripheral smear; Auer rods identified
- Hemoglobin: 6.8 g/dL (severely low)
- Hematocrit: 21%
- Platelets: 32,000/uL (severely low)
- Absolute neutrophil count: markedly reduced (most WBC represent blasts)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138, Potassium 3.9, Chloride 102, Bicarbonate 22
- BUN 14, Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m²
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
- AST 38 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L
- Total protein 6.8 g/dL, Albumin 3.6 g/dL
- Glucose 95 mg/dL
- LDH: 892 U/L (elevated, consistent with high tumor burden)
- Uric acid: 7.8 mg/dL (mildly elevated, tumor lysis risk)

Coagulation:
- PT 13.8 sec, INR 1.1
- PTT 32 sec
- Fibrinogen 310 mg/dL
- D-dimer: mildly elevated at 1.2 ug/mL FEU (monitoring for DIC)

HLA Typing: Sent on patient and one full sibling

Blood cultures: Pending; obtained prior to any antibiotics

TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine)

Pre-chemotherapy Echocardiogram:
- LVEF: 62%, normal left ventricular size and function
- No significant valvular disease
- No pericardial effusion
- Adequate cardiac reserve for anthracycline-based therapy

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (obtained at admission for staging):
- No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy
- Small bilateral pleural effusions (trace to small), likely from hypoalbuminemia and high tumor burden
- Splenomegaly: spleen 14.2 cm (craniocaudal dimension)
- Hepatomegaly: liver 17.1 cm, no discrete lesions
- No abdominal or pelvic lymphadenopathy
- No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions
- Impression: hepatosplenomegaly consistent with leukemic infiltration; no discrete masses; no osseous involvement

Brain MRI with and without contrast:
- No leptomeningeal enhancement
- No parenchymal lesions or masses
- No evidence of intracranial leukostasis or CNS leukemic infiltration
- Normal study

---

BONE MARROW BIOPSY AND MOLECULAR RESULTS (summary)

Bone marrow biopsy (right posterior iliac crest): Hypercellular marrow (~95%) with 72% myeloblasts. Auer rods present. Flow cytometry: AML immunophenotype (CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+, HLA-DR+; B and T lineage markers negative). Cytogenetics: 46,XX, normal karyotype.

NGS (523-gene comprehensive myeloid panel):
- FLT3-ITD: exon 14, 33 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.68, VAF 40.2% — HIGH allelic ratio, clinically actionable, indicates eligibility for FLT3 inhibitor therapy
- DNMT3A p.R882H: VAF 48.1% — pathogenic, likely founder/clonal mutation
- RUNX1 p.S291fs*16: VAF 43.7% — pathogenic, associated with adverse outcomes
- TET2 p.Q916*: VAF 22.4% — pathogenic, likely subclonal
- PTPN11 p.E76K: VAF 8.3% — likely pathogenic, RAS pathway
- NPM1: NEGATIVE (important — FLT3-ITD in NPM1-unmutated context confers adverse prognosis)
- IDH1/IDH2: NEGATIVE
- TP53: NEGATIVE
- ASXL1: NEGATIVE
- No pathognomonic fusions (BCR-ABL1, PML-RARA, RUNX1-RUNX1T1, CBFB-MYH11 all negative)

ELN 2022 Risk Classification: ADVERSE

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old woman presenting with de novo AML with a high-risk molecular profile. She has no prior hematologic disorder or antecedent myelodysplastic syndrome. Her disease is characterized by high-allelic-ratio FLT3-ITD in the context of NPM1-unmutated normal-karyotype AML, with co-occurring DNMT3A R882H and RUNX1 frameshift mutations — a combination that places her firmly in the ELN 2022 Adverse risk category and is associated with a high risk of relapse even after achieving complete remission with standard induction. There is no evidence of CNS involvement, no leukostasis clinically, and she has adequate organ function and ECOG PS 1, making her an appropriate candidate for intensive induction chemotherapy.

1. INDUCTION CHEMOTHERAPY: We will proceed with standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy: cytarabine 100 mg/m² per day via continuous intravenous infusion on days 1 through 7, plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m² IV on days 1, 2, and 3. Given her FLT3-ITD mutation with high allelic ratio, we will add midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8 through 21 per the RATIFY trial protocol (Stone et al., NEJM 2017), which demonstrated improved OS and EFS when midostaurin was added to 7+3 induction in FLT3-mutated AML. Chemotherapy to begin today following administration of the second unit of packed red blood cells and following pre-hydration for tumor lysis prophylaxis. LVEF confirmed adequate at 62% by pre-treatment echo, permitting full-dose anthracycline.

2. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND TUMOR LYSIS PROPHYLAXIS: Allopurinol 300 mg PO daily initiated. Aggressive IV hydration per protocol. Uric acid and electrolytes monitored every 6-8 hours for the first 48-72 hours. Transfusion thresholds: pRBC for hemoglobin <8 g/dL, platelets for count <10,000/uL (or <20,000/uL with active bleeding/fever). Anti-emetics: ondansetron plus dexamethasone per standard protocol; midostaurin-related nausea is anticipated and additional agents (prochlorperazine, lorazepam) will be available as needed. Growth factor support (G-CSF) will be withheld during and immediately following induction per standard practice, as it may interfere with leukemia clearance.

3. INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS: Antifungal prophylaxis with micafungin will be initiated at onset of neutropenia. Acyclovir for HSV prophylaxis. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PJP prophylaxis will be deferred until count recovery per standard protocol. Surveillance blood cultures if febrile. Empiric broad-spectrum gram-negative coverage (e.g., cefepime) for febrile neutropenia episodes per institutional protocol.

4. TRANSPLANT PLANNING — INITIATED NOW: Given her adverse ELN risk profile (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated, RUNX1 mutation, DNMT3A mutation), we strongly recommend proceeding directly to allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (alloHSCT) in first complete remission, should she respond to induction. The decision to forego consolidation chemotherapy in favor of expedited transplant is appropriate in this risk category. HLA typing has been sent today on the patient. Consent obtained to contact her one full sibling (age 52, male, healthy) for HLA typing. Simultaneously, we will initiate an unrelated donor search through the National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP/Be The Match) registry, given the possibility that the sibling may not be a full match. The search will proceed in parallel with induction.

5. BONE MARROW RESPONSE ASSESSMENT: Bone marrow biopsy will be scheduled for day 28 post-induction start to assess for complete remission. MRD assessment by 8-color flow cytometry will be performed at that time, in addition to cytogenetics and molecular PCR for FLT3-ITD. If CR with MRD negativity is achieved, we will proceed expeditiously to transplant planning.

6. ONGOING MONITORING: CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel with LDH daily during induction. Coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer) every 48 hours given mild coagulopathy at baseline, to monitor for DIC. Tacrolimus/immunosuppression not yet applicable but relevant post-transplant. Thyroid function monitoring given levothyroxine use and anticipated physiologic stress.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND FAMILY SUPPORT: This is a devastating diagnosis for a previously healthy 48-year-old. I spent considerable time discussing the diagnosis, the treatment plan, expected course of hospitalization (minimum 4-5 weeks for induction), risks and expected complications (febrile neutropenia, mucositis, transfusion dependence), and the overall treatment strategy including transplant planning. Ms. Chen is a highly educated individual who asked thoughtful questions and demonstrated excellent understanding of the information discussed. Her husband was present and was also deeply engaged. Social work has been consulted. A care conference with her family will be arranged. Fertility counseling was briefly discussed — given the urgency of treatment and the myeloablative conditioning that will follow, fertility preservation is unfortunately not feasible at this juncture without delaying potentially life-saving therapy. This was acknowledged by the patient.

8. FINANCIAL COUNSELING: Financial counseling and insurance authorization processes for midostaurin have been initiated. Patient assistance programs noted as available if needed.

Overall prognosis: Ms. Chen has high-risk AML. With intensive induction and consolidative alloHSCT in first CR, long-term disease-free survival is achievable, though her ELN Adverse risk profile with FLT3-ITD AR >0.5 and co-occurring RUNX1 mutation confers meaningful relapse risk. The goal remains cure. We have communicated this clearly and honestly to the patient and her family.

Plan discussed with the attending hematologist/oncologist [name], who is in agreement with the above assessment and plan.

Electronically signed,
Hematology/Oncology Fellow
Co-signed: Attending Hematologist/Oncologist
Inpatient Hematology/Oncology Service
